normalized_question_text:
The figure illustrates a cascaded system of two water tanks. Water flows:
- into the first tank at a rate \( r_0(t) \),
- out of the first tank and into the second at a rate \( r_1(t) \), and
- out of the second tank at a rate \( r_2(t) \).
The rate of flow out of each tank is proportional to the height of the water in that tank: \( r_1(t) = k_1 h_1(t) \) and \( r_2(t) = k_2 h_2(t) \), where \( k_1 \) and \( k_2 \) are each \( 0.2 \, \text{m}^2/\text{second} \). Both tanks have heights of 1m. The cross-sectional area of tank 1 is \( A_1 = 4 \, \text{m}^2 \) and that of the second tank is \( A_2 = 2 \, \text{m}^2 \). At time \( t = 0 \), both tanks are empty. Assume that \( r_0(t) \) is held constant at rate \( r_0 \).
What is the maximum value of \( r_0 \) such that neither tank will ever overflow if both tanks start out empty?
\( r_0 = \) ________ m\(^3\)/s

sentence_segments.text: The figure illustrates a cascaded system of two water tanks.
Water flows:
- into the first tank at a rate \( r_0(t) \),
- out of the first tank and into the second at a rate \( r_1(t) \), and
- out of the second tank at a rate \( r_2(t) \).

The rate of flow out of each tank is proportional to the height of the water in that tank: \( r_1(t) = k_1 h_1(t) \) and \( r_2(t) = k_2 h_2(t) \), where \( k_1 \) and \( k_2 \) are each \( 0.2 \, \text{m}^2/\text{second} \).
Both tanks have heights of 1m.

At time \( t = 0 \), both tanks are empty.
Assume that \( r_0(t) \) is held constant at rate \( r_0 \).
What is the maximum value of \( r_0 \) such that neither tank will ever overflow if both tanks start out empty?
